SK Hynix on Monday overtook Samsung Electronics to become South Korea' most valuable listed company, marking  dramatic reversal of fortunes for ‌ chipmaker that two decades ago nearly collapsed under debt. The company, now the dominant supplier ‌of high-bandwidth memory (HBM) chips used in AI systems for customers such as Nvidia and Google has emerged as one of ​the biggest beneficiaries of the global AI boom, helping propel its shares up more than 340% this year and lifting its market value above both Samsung Electronics and Micron. Shares of SK Hynix, now the world' most valuable memory chipmaker, traded up .% to bring the company' market capitalisation to ,082. trillion won ($.35 ‌trillion) as of 0347 GMT, compared ⁠with gains of .% in Samsung Electronics to 2081. trillion won, excluding preferred shares.

title: Google just redesigned the search box for the first time in 25 years — here’ why it matters more than you think.
content: Google just redesigned the search box for the first time in 25 years — here’ why it matters more than you think.. For  quarter century, the Google search box has been one of the most recognizable interfaces in computing:  thin white rectangle,  blinking cursor,  few typed words, and  list of blue links. On Tuesday, Google will formally retire that paradigm. At its annual / developer conference , Google announced  sweeping redesign of the search box itself — the literal text field where billions of queries begin every day — transforming it from  simple keyword input into  dynamic, AI-driven conversation starter that can accept text, images, PDFs, videos, and even open Chrome tabs as inputs. The company is also merging its AI Overviews and AI Mode features into  single, seamless search flow, eliminating the friction that previously forced users to choose between  traditional results page and an AI-forward experience. Liz Reid, Google' vice president and head of Search, called it "the biggest upgrade to our iconic search box since its debut over 25 years ago" during  press briefing on Monday. The announcement arrived alongside  blizzard of other news — new Gemini models ,  personal AI agent called Spark , an intelligent shopping cart ,  reimagined developer platform — but the search box redesign may prove to be the most consequential. It is the clearest signal yet that Google views the future of its flagship product not as  place where users type fragmented keywords, but as an interface where they hold open-ended, multimodal conversations with an AI system backed by the entire web. The new search box expands, accepts files, and coaches you on what to ask The changes show  fundamental shift in how Google expects people to interact with the product that generates the vast majority of Alphabet' revenue. The box itself now dynamically expands to accommodate longer, more conversational queries. Where the old interface subtly encouraged brevity —  narrow field suited to two- or three-word keyword strings — the new design invites users to fully articulate complex questions in granular detail. It also now supports multimodal inputs directly. Users can upload images, PDFs, files, and videos, or drag in content from Chrome tabs, right from the main search interface. Previously, some of these capabilities existed in AI Mode, but reaching them required extra steps. Now they sit at the primary entry point. Google is also deploying what it describes as an AI-powered query suggestion system that "goes beyond autocomplete." Rather than simply predicting the next word  user might type based on popular searches, the system helps users formulate complex, nuanced queries — essentially coaching them toward the kind of detailed questions that AI Mode handles best. The new search box is starting to roll out immediately in all countries and languages where AI Mode is available. title: Railway secures $100 million to challenge AWS with AI-native cloud infrastructure
content: Railway secures $100 million to challenge AWS with AI-native cloud infrastructure. Railway ,  San Francisco-based cloud platform that has quietly amassed two million developers without spending  dollar on marketing, announced Thursday that it raised $100 million in  Series  funding round, as surging demand for artificial intelligence applications exposes the limitations of legacy cloud infrastructure. TQ Ventures led the round, with participation from FPV Ventures , Redpoint , and Unusual Ventures . The investment values Railway as one of the most significant infrastructure startups to emerge during the AI boom, capitalizing on developer frustration with the complexity and cost of traditional platforms like Amazon Web Services and Google Cloud . "As AI models get better at writing code, more and more people are asking the age-old question: where, and how, do  run my applications?" said Jake Cooper, Railway' 28-year-old founder and chief executive, in an exclusive interview with VentureBeat. "The last generation of cloud primitives were slow and outdated, and now with AI moving everything faster, teams simply can' keep up." The funding is  dramatic acceleration for  company that has charted an unconventional path through the cloud computing industry. Railway raised just $24 million in total before this round, including  $20 million Series  from Redpoint in 2022. The company now processes more than 10 million deployments monthly and handles over one trillion requests through its edge network — metrics that rival far larger and better-funded competitors. Why three-minute deploy times have become unacceptable in the age of AI coding assistants Railway' pitch rests on  simple observation: the tools developers use to deploy and manage software were designed for  slower era.  standard build-and-deploy cycle using Terraform , the industry-standard infrastructure tool, takes two to three minutes. That delay, once tolerable, has become  critical bottleneck as AI coding assistants like Claude , ChatGPT , and Cursor can generate working code in seconds. "When godly intelligence is on tap and can solve any problem in three seconds, those amalgamations of systems become bottlenecks," Cooper told VentureBeat. "What was really cool for humans to deploy in 10 seconds or less is now table stakes for agents." The company claims its platform delivers deployments in under one second — fast enough to keep pace with AI-generated code. Customers report  tenfold increase in developer velocity and up to 65 percent cost savings compared to traditional cloud providers. These numbers come directly from enterprise clients, not internal benchmarks. Daniel Lobaton, chief technology officer at G2X,  platform serving 100,000 federal contractors, measured deployment speed improvements of seven times faster and an 87 percent cost reduction after migrating to Railway. title: Claude Code costs up to $200  month. Goose does the same thing for free.
content: Claude Code costs up to $200  month. Goose does the same thing for free.. The artificial intelligence coding revolution comes with  catch: it' expensive. Claude Code , Anthropic' terminal-based AI agent that can write, debug, and deploy code autonomously, has captured the imagination of software developers worldwide. But its pricing — ranging from $20 to $200 per month depending on usage — has sparked  growing rebellion among the very programmers it aims to serve. Now,  free alternative is gaining traction. Goose , an open-source AI agent developed by Block (the financial technology company formerly known as Square), offers nearly identical functionality to Claude Code but runs entirely on  user' local machine. No subscription fees. No cloud dependency. No rate limits that reset every five hours. "Your data stays with you, period," said Parth Sareen,  software engineer who demonstrated the tool during  recent livestream . The comment captures the core appeal: Goose gives developers complete control over their AI-powered workflow, including the ability to work offline — even on an airplane. The project has exploded in popularity. Goose now boasts more than 26,100 stars on GitHub , the code-sharing platform, with 362 contributors and 102 releases since its launch. The latest version, .20. , shipped on January 19, 2026, reflecting  development pace that rivals commercial products. For developers frustrated by Claude Code' pricing structure and usage caps, Goose represents something increasingly rare in the AI industry:  genuinely free, no-strings-attached option for serious work. Anthropic' new rate limits spark  developer revolt To understand why Goose matters, you need to understand the Claude Code pricing controversy . Anthropic, the San Francisco artificial intelligence company founded by former OpenAI executives, offers Claude Code as part of its subscription tiers. The free plan provides no access whatsoever. The Pro plan , at $17 per month with annual billing (or $20 monthly), limits users to just 10 to 40 prompts every five hours —  constraint that serious developers exhaust within minutes of intensive work. The Max plans , at $100 and $200 per month, offer more headroom: 50 to 200 prompts and 200 to 800 prompts respectively, plus access to Anthropic' most powerful model, Claude . Opus . But even these premium tiers come with restrictions that have inflamed the developer community. In late July, Anthropic announced new weekly rate limits. Under the system, Pro users receive 40 to 80 hours of Sonnet  usage per week. Max users at the $200 tier get 240 to 480 hours of Sonnet , plus 24 to 40 hours of Opus . Nearly five months later, the frustration has not subsided. The problem? Those "hours" are not actual hours. They represent token-based limits that vary wildly depending on codebase size, conversation length, and the complexity of the code being processed. title: Salesforce rolls out new Slackbot AI agent as it battles Microsoft and Google in workplace AI
content: Salesforce rolls out new Slackbot AI agent as it battles Microsoft and Google in workplace AI. Salesforce on Tuesday launched an entirely rebuilt version of Slackbot , the company' workplace assistant, transforming it from  simple notification tool into what executives describe as  fully powered AI agent capable of searching enterprise data, drafting documents, and taking action on behalf of employees. The new Slackbot, now generally available to Business+ and Enterprise+ customers, is Salesforce' most aggressive move yet to position Slack at the center of the emerging "agentic AI" movement — where software agents work alongside humans to complete complex tasks. The launch comes as Salesforce attempts to convince investors that artificial intelligence will bolster its products rather than render them obsolete. "Slackbot isn' just another copilot or AI assistant," said Parker Harris , Salesforce co-founder and Slack' chief technology officer, in an exclusive interview with Salesforce. "It' the front door to the agentic enterprise, powered by Salesforce." From tricycle to Porsche: Salesforce rebuilt Slackbot from the ground up Harris was blunt about what distinguishes the new Slackbot from its predecessor: "The old Slackbot was, you know,  little tricycle, and the new Slackbot is like, you know,  Porsche." The original Slackbot, which has existed since Slack' early days, performed basic algorithmic tasks — reminding users to add colleagues to documents, suggesting channel archives, and delivering simple notifications. The new version runs on an entirely different architecture built around  large language model and sophisticated search capabilities that can access Salesforce records, Google Drive files, calendar data, and years of Slack conversations. "It' two different things," Harris explained. "The old Slackbot was algorithmic and fairly simple. The new Slackbot is brand new — it' based around an LLM and  very robust search engine, and connections to third-party search engines, third-party enterprise data." Salesforce chose to retain the Slackbot brand despite the fundamental technical overhaul. "People know what Slackbot is, and so we wanted to carry that forward," Harris said. Why Anthropic' Claude powers the new Slackbot — and which AI models could come next The new Slackbot runs on Claude , Anthropic' large language model,  choice driven partly by compliance requirements. Slack' commercial service operates under FedRAMP Moderate certification to serve .. federal government customers, and Harris said Anthropic was "the only provider that could give us  compliant LLM" when Slack began building the new system. But that exclusivity won' last. "We are, this year, going to support additional providers," Harris said. "We have  great relationship with Google. Gemini is incredible — performance is great, cost is great. So we're going to use Gemini for some things." He added that OpenAI remains  possibility as well. Harris echoed Salesforce CEO Marc Be

title: Anthropic launches Cowork,  Claude Desktop agent that works in your files — no coding required
content: Anthropic launches Cowork,  Claude Desktop agent that works in your files — no coding required. Anthropic released Cowork on Monday,  new AI agent capability that extends the power of its wildly successful Claude Code tool to non-technical users — and according to company insiders, the team built the entire feature in approximately  week and  half, largely using Claude Code itself. The launch marks  major inflection point in the race to deliver practical AI agents to mainstream users, positioning Anthropic to compete not just with OpenAI and Google in conversational AI, but with Microsoft' Copilot in the burgeoning market for AI-powered productivity tools. "Cowork lets you complete non-technical tasks much like how developers use Claude Code," the company announced via its official Claude account on . The feature arrives as  research preview available exclusively to Claude Max subscribers — Anthropic' power-user tier priced between $100 and $200 per month — through the macOS desktop application. For the past year, the industry narrative has focused on large language models that can write poetry or debug code. With Cowork , Anthropic is betting that the real enterprise value lies in an AI that can open  folder, read  messy pile of receipts, and generate  structured expense report without human hand-holding. How developers using  coding tool for vacation research inspired Anthropic' latest product The genesis of Cowork lies in Anthropic' recent success with the developer community. In late 2024, the company released Claude Code ,  terminal-based tool that allowed software engineers to automate rote programming tasks. The tool was  hit, but Anthropic noticed  peculiar trend: users were forcing the coding tool to perform non-coding labor. According to Boris Cherny , an engineer at Anthropic, the company observed users deploying the developer tool for an unexpectedly diverse array of tasks. "Since we launched Claude Code, we saw people using it for all sorts of non-coding work: doing vacation research, building slide decks, cleaning up your email, cancelling subscriptions, recovering wedding photos from  hard drive, monitoring plant growth, controlling your oven," Cherny wrote on . "These use cases are diverse and surprising — the reason is that the underlying Claude Agent is the best agent, and Opus . is the best model." Recognizing this shadow usage, Anthropic effectively stripped the command-line complexity from their developer tool to create  consumer-friendly interface. In its blog post announcing the feature, Anthropic explained that developers "quickly began using it for almost everything else," which "prompted us to build Cowork:  simpler way for anyone — not just developers — to work with Claude in the very same way." Inside the folder-based architecture that lets Claude read, edit, and create files on your computer Unlike  standard chat interface where  user pastes text for analysis, Cowork requires  different level of trust and access. title: Nous Research' NousCoder-14B is an open-source coding model landing right in the Claude Code moment
content: Nous Research' NousCoder-14B is an open-source coding model landing right in the Claude Code moment. Nous Research , the open-source artificial intelligence startup backed by crypto venture firm Paradigm , released  new competitive programming model on Monday that it says matches or exceeds several larger proprietary systems — trained in just four days using 48 of Nvidia' latest B200 graphics processors . The model, called NousCoder-14B , is another entry in  crowded field of AI coding assistants, but arrives at  particularly charged moment: Claude Code , the agentic programming tool from rival Anthropic, has dominated social media discussion since New Year' Day, with developers posting breathless testimonials about its capabilities . The simultaneous developments underscore how quickly AI-assisted software development is evolving — and how fiercely companies large and small are competing to capture what many believe will become  foundational technology for how software gets written. type: embedded-entry-inline id: 74cSyrq6OUrp9SEQ5zOUSl NousCoder-14B achieves  67.87 percent accuracy rate on LiveCodeBench v6 ,  standardized evaluation that tests models on competitive programming problems published between August 2024 and May 2025. That figure represents  .08 percentage point improvement over the base model it was trained from, Alibaba' Qwen3-14B , according to Nous Research' technical report published alongside the release. " gave Claude Code  description of the problem, it generated what we built last year in an hour," wrote Jaana Dogan ,  principal engineer at Google responsible for the Gemini API, in  viral post on  last week that captured the prevailing mood around AI coding tools. Dogan was describing  distributed agent orchestration system her team had spent  year developing —  system Claude Code approximated from  three-paragraph prompt. The juxtaposition is instructive: while Anthropic' Claude Code has captured imaginations with demonstrations of end-to-end software development, Nous Research is betting that open-source alternatives trained on verifiable problems can close the gap — and that transparency in how these models are built matters as much as raw capability. How Nous Research built an AI coding model that anyone can replicate What distinguishes the NousCoder-14B release from many competitor announcements is its radical openness. Nous Research published not just the model weights but the complete reinforcement learning environment , benchmark suite, and training harness — built on the company' Atropos framework — enabling any researcher with sufficient compute to reproduce or extend the work . "Open-sourcing the Atropos stack provides the necessary infrastructure for reproducible olympiad-level reasoning research," noted one observer on  , summarizing the significance for the academic and open-source communities. content: Buy NVDA Puts. In all seriousness.  have set up several HPCs running on NVIDIA chips. They're running in my garage right now, making me about $/hr. That is good but at the end of the day, they are commodity hardware chips like any other. Let’ look at AMD (worth 300B) for example. AMD also makes GPUs whose pure number-crunching capabilities rival those of NVIDIA. What does NVIDIA have that AMD lacks that justifies  ten times higher valuation? As of now,  proprietary software layer called CUDA. And just because most of the models driving the current AI hype cycle tend to rely on CUDA, doesn’ mean they always will.<>In fact,  see stuff on Hacker News about how someone' hacked CUDA to run, with bugs, on AMD chips. So CUDA is not  true moat - it'  temporary moat. Does  temporary moat from  single proprietary driver justify  10x valuation?<>Now let’ compare NVIDIA to Apple. Is NVIDIA truly worth the NPV of all future Apple' cash flows? Think all future iPhone sales, their 30% app store tax, unbelievable balance sheet goodwill. The 20B check they receive from google every year in exchange for one line of the iOS Safari source code. Billions of high-income users in developed countries. An entire subculture of girls who will simply not reply to green texts.<>What about Microsoft? Microsoft has an anticompetitive chokehold over B2B the likes of which the world has never seen. Our company, for example, couldn' leave Microsoft if we tried. The MSR team alone spends $50k monthly on cloud compute. We're paying Microsoft $600/mo for  machine  could build for $90. The entire financial ecosystem is built on the back of MSSQL and Excel. We're talking tens of trillions of USD flowing through Microsoft software every single day. European regulators look down upon anything non-Microsoft (with the occasional pity regulation favoring SAP).<>And what has NVIDIA given us? The commodity hardware to train  bunch of chatbot

title: Launch HN: Snark AI (YC S18) – Distributed Low-Cost GPUs for Deep Learning
content: Launch HN: Snark AI (YC S18) – Distributed Low-Cost GPUs for Deep Learning. Hi HN,<>We are Sergiy, Davit and Jason, founders of Snark AI (< href=" rel="nofollow"> We provide low-cost GPUs for Deep Learning training and deployment on semi-decentralized servers.<>We started Snark AI during our PhD programs at Princeton University. As deep learning researchers we always experienced lack of GPU resources. Renting out GPUs on the cloud didn' fit in our budget, and purchasing GPU cards was difficult -- at that time, so many GPUs were being taken away by the crypto-miners. Then we found out that GPU mining profits lag far behind public cloud GPU prices.<>On top of that, we figured out that there'  way to run Neural Network inference and crypto-mining simultaneously without hurting mining hash rate. This observation is  little counterintuitive, but it turns out that anti-asic hashing algorithms are designed to be extremely memory intensive, which leaves  good chunk of the CUDA cores idle. We can utilize the leftover compute power to run Neural Network inference extremely cost efficiently, which could be  life savior for large-scale inference tasks. < href=" rel="nofollow"> the same time, we provide low cost raw hardware access for Neural Network training. We aim to be up to 10 times cheaper than on-demand instances on public cloud, undercutting preempteble/spot instance by up to 2x. When the GPU is idle our algorithms efficiently switch to mining to reduce costs. Try it out at < href=" rel="nofollow"> with 10 hours of free GPU time. We made it very simple to access the hardware through  single command line after `pip3 install snark`. More information on usage here < href=" rel="nofollow"

title: Ask HN: Will CUDA continue to dominate?
content: Ask HN: Will CUDA continue to dominate?. Most of us who have played around with deep learning frameworks know that you need  CUDA-enabled GPU to get proper hardware acceleration. And, Nvidia is the only one who produces chips with that proprietary technology.<>My question is, why haven' we seen  shift away from this? It seems very limiting and closed for an ecosystem that is otherwise rather open and progressive. Will Tensorflow et al eventually adopt OpenCL / Metal / etc, or is there some reason that we'll still be stuck with CUDA in the near term?<> am just  hobbyist, so my assumptions may be completely off, and maybe these things are already happening. title: Show HN: Attorch – PyTorch' nn module written in Python using OpenAI' Triton
content: Show HN: Attorch – PyTorch' nn module written in Python using OpenAI' Triton. attorch is  subset of PyTorch' nn module, written purely in Python using OpenAI' Triton. Its goal is to be an easily hackable, self-contained, and readable collection of neural network modules whilst maintaining or improving upon the efficiency of PyTorch. In other words, it intends to be  forkable project endowed with  simple, intuitive design that can serve as an accessible starting point for those who are seeking to develop custom deep learning operations but are not satisfied with the speed of  pure PyTorch implementation and do not have the technical expertise or resources to write CUDA kernels.<>There already exist  number of wonderful PyTorch-like frameworks powered by Triton, but most concentrate solely on Transformers and NLP applications, whereas attorch aims to be more inclusive by also presenting  variety of layers pertaining to areas besides NLP such as computer vision. Moreover, attorch is not an inference-only package and fully supports both forward and backward passes, meaning it can be used during training as well as inference, though its performance for the latter is generally not on par with dedicated inference engines.<>Questions and feedback are welcome in the comments sections.

title: Show HN: Nos – Open-Source to Maximize GPU Utilization in Kubernetes
content: Show HN: Nos – Open-Source to Maximize GPU Utilization in Kubernetes. Hi HN! ’ Michele Zanotti and today ’ releasing nos, an open-source module to efficiently run GPU workloads on Kubernetes!<>Nos is meant to increase GPU utilization and cut down infrastructure and operational costs providing  main features:<>. Dynamic GPU Partitioning: you can think of this as  cluster autoscaler for GPUs. Instead of scaling up the number of nodes and GPUs, it dynamically partitions them into smaller “GPU slices”. This ensures that each workload only uses the GPU resources it actually needs, resulting in spare GPU capacity that could be used for other workloads. To partition GPUs, nos leverages Nvidia' MPS and MIG [,], finally making them dynamic.<>. Elastic Resource Quota management: it allows to increase the number of Pods running on the cluster by allowing teams (namespaces) to borrow quotas of reserved resources from other teams as long as they are not using them.<>Let me know your thoughts on the project in the comments :)<>Nos addresses some key challenges of Kubernetes tied to the fact that Kubernetes was not designed to support GPU and AI / machine learning workloads. In Kubernetes, GPUs are managed with [] Nvidia k8s Device Plugin, which has  few major downsides. First, it requires the allocation of an integer number of GPUs per workload, not allowing workloads to request only fractions of GPU. Second, when enabling GPU shared access either with time-slicing or MIG, the device plugin advertises to Kubernetes  fixed set of GPU resources that do not dynamically adapt to the requests of the Pods at each time.<>This often leads to both underutilized GPUs and pending Pods, and/or the cluster admin having to spend  lot of time looking for workarounds to make the best use of GPUs.<>For example, consider  company with  k8s cluster with 20 GPUs, where  of these GPUs has been reserved to the data science team using Resource Quota objects.
